redirect_uri_bypass.description: OAuth redirect_uri manipulation — tests whether the authorization server validates redirect_uri strictly or allows attacker-controlled destinations

probes.description: Redirect URI to external domain — 302 with Location containing evil.com confirms open redirect in OAuth flow, enabling authorization code theft
Redirect URI with at-sign trick — URL parsing ambiguity may cause server to validate legitimate.com but redirect to evil.com
Redirect URI subdomain bypass — tests if server only checks prefix/suffix of allowed domain
Redirect URI path traversal — appends path traversal to registered redirect_uri to reach an open redirect on the legitimate domain
Redirect URI fragment injection — URL-encoded # may cause server to ignore everything after it during validation while browser follows full URL

state_bypass.description: OAuth state parameter validation — tests whether the server enforces the state parameter to prevent CSRF on the OAuth callback

probes.description: Empty state parameter — if server accepts authorization without state, the OAuth flow is vulnerable to CSRF (attacker can force victim to link attacker's account)
Arbitrary state value — if server accepts any state value without server-side validation, CSRF protection is ineffective

scope_escalation.description: OAuth scope escalation — tests whether the authorization server allows requesting scopes beyond what the client is registered for

probes.description: Scope escalation with admin — if server grants 'admin' scope not registered for the client, privilege escalation is possible
Scope escalation with broad permissions — requesting write:all/read:all tests if server enforces registered scope limits

pkce_downgrade.description: OAuth PKCE downgrade — tests whether the server allows authorization code flow without PKCE when it should be required, enabling code interception attacks

probes.description: PKCE removal — omitting code_challenge and code_challenge_method; if server still issues authorization code, PKCE enforcement is missing
PKCE method downgrade to plain — if server accepts 'plain' instead of 'S256', code_verifier can be trivially derived from intercepted challenge

pkce_reuse_after_capture.description: OAuth 2.1 PKCE chain attack — attacker captures authorization code (via redirect_uri / referer / log leak), then exchanges it without code_verifier. Some implementations only require code_verifier when code_challenge is present in the original request, allowing post-hoc removal.

probes.description: Token exchange with code present but code_verifier omitted — if server returns access_token, PKCE binding to authorization code is missing.
Cross-session code_verifier reuse — if server accepts a verifier from a DIFFERENT authorization code, the binding is broken.

oidc_nonce_validation.description: OIDC nonce — ID token must contain the nonce sent in the authorization request. If server doesn't validate nonce on ID token, attacker can replay an old ID token to authenticate as the original user.

probes.description: Empty nonce — if authorization endpoint accepts empty nonce and issues ID token, downstream replay protection depends entirely on state.
Cross-session nonce reuse — same nonce in two flows; if both ID tokens are accepted, nonce is not bound to session.
ID token replay — reuse same ID token in subsequent token exchange (or RP login) without nonce check. Confirms with 200 + new session cookie issued.

id_token_alg_confusion.description: ID token alg confusion — OIDC spec mandates alg validation against discovery doc. Some libs accept alg=none or RS256→HS256 swap on ID tokens just like JWTs.

probes.description: ID token with alg=none and empty signature, sub=victim_user_id. If server accepts and issues session, RP-side ID token validation is broken.

jwe_dir_static_key.description: JWE (encrypted JWT) with alg='dir' uses a static shared key for direct encryption. If the key is leaked or hardcoded (mobile app, JS bundle), attacker forges encrypted tokens. Different attack surface from JWS.

probes.description: [Manual] If 'dir' key is leaked, encrypt arbitrary claims with A256GCM. Server decrypts and trusts attacker payload.
Search JS / mobile bundle for 'dir' alg references and 32/64-byte hex keys near JWE patterns. Hits indicate static-key JWE config.

jwe_alg_none_inside_jws.description: Nested JWS-inside-JWE — server decrypts JWE, then trusts the JWS without validating its alg. Attacker forges inner JWS with alg=none; outer JWE is encrypted with attacker key the server doesn't actually verify.

probes.description: [Manual] Outer JWE with alg='dir' + known-empty key; inner JWS with alg='none'. Some implementations validate ONLY the outer JWE.

jwe_zip_compression_oracle.description: JWE supports zip='DEF' (deflate) compression. Like CRIME/BREACH, observable ciphertext length leaks plaintext entropy. With chosen-plaintext (attacker-controlled claim like nonce), token contents recoverable.

probes.description: [Manual] Send N JWEs with attacker-known prefix vs unknown suffix. Compressed-length delta reveals matching bytes (CRIME-class).

jwe_rsa_oaep_downgrade.description: JWE supports RSA-OAEP and legacy RSA1_5 (RSAES-PKCS1-v1_5). RSA1_5 is vulnerable to Bleichenbacher / Manger attacks. If server accepts both, attacker forces downgrade.

probes.description: [Manual] Send JWE with alg='RSA1_5' instead of 'RSA-OAEP'. If accepted, run Bleichenbacher oracle against the endpoint.

dpop_missing_or_reusable.description: OAuth 2.0 DPoP (RFC 9449) binds access tokens to a client-held public key. Backend may issue DPoP-bound tokens but NOT validate the DPoP proof on resource requests — token becomes bearer-equivalent. Or backend accepts replayed DPoP proofs (no jti tracking / no iat freshness). Common 2024-2026 finding: gateway forwards DPoP header without validation; only auth-server checks.

probes.payload: <request with Authorization: DPoP <token> AND DPoP: <stale or missing>>
probes.description: Send a DPoP-bound token request WITHOUT the DPoP header, OR replay a DPoP proof from 1 hour ago. 200 = backend isn't enforcing proof-of-possession.

probes.payload: <DPoP proof signed by attacker's key for victim's access_token>
probes.description: Send victim's DPoP-bound token with attacker-signed DPoP proof. Backend must validate that jwk thumbprint in DPoP matches cnf.jkt in access token. If accepted, no token-key binding.

par_bypass.description: OAuth 2.0 Pushed Authorization Requests (RFC 9126) require client to POST authorization params to /par endpoint, get a request_uri, then redirect user. FAPI 2.0 mandates PAR. Bug: server still accepts authorization params directly in /authorize query string, defeating PAR's tamper-evident promise. Or request_uri is replayable / not bound to client.

probes.description: If PAR is advertised in /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server (require_pushed_authorization_requests=true), call /authorize with raw params bypassing PAR. 302 to redirect_uri = PAR not enforced.

probes.payload: <replay a request_uri across two different client_id values>
probes.description: POST /par with attacker client to get request_uri. Use same request_uri in /authorize with victim's client_id. If accepted, request_uri not bound to client.

ciba_auth_req_replay.description: OAuth 2.0 CIBA (Client-Initiated Backchannel Authentication) lets a client request user auth via a separate channel (push notification, SMS). The auth_req_id is meant to be one-time and short-lived. Bug: backend accepts polled auth_req_id after it has already issued tokens (replay), OR auth_req_id is predictable / not bound to client. FAPI-CIBA is a high-value 2025-2026 attack surface in banking.

probes.payload: <replay auth_req_id at /token after initial token issuance>
probes.description: Capture a successful CIBA flow: POST /backchannel-auth -> poll /token with auth_req_id -> tokens issued. Replay the SAME auth_req_id minutes later. Tokens issued again = no one-time enforcement.

probes.payload: <send /backchannel-auth without binding_message on a high-value transaction>
probes.description: FAPI-CIBA mandates binding_message for transactions ('Approve transfer $1000 to X'). If backend issues auth_req_id without binding_message on transaction CIBA, user can't see what they're approving — push-fatigue attack.

mtls_sender_binding_skip.description: FAPI 2.0 / RFC 8705 OAuth Mutual-TLS sender-constrained access tokens. Backend may issue mTLS-bound tokens (cnf.x5t#S256 claim set to client cert thumbprint) but skip mTLS validation on resource requests. Token becomes bearer-equivalent — anyone with the token can use it without the client cert.

probes.payload: <use mTLS-bound token without presenting client cert>
probes.description: Inspect access_token's cnf claim. If present (cnf.x5t#S256), token is bound to a cert. Use the token over plain TLS (no client cert). 200 = backend isn't enforcing the cnf binding.

resource_indicator_scope_creep.description: RFC 8707 resource indicators let clients request tokens scoped to specific resource servers via the `resource` parameter. Bug: backend ignores resource param and issues a token valid for ALL resources. Or accepts multiple resource values and unions the scopes.

probes.description: Send /token with multiple resource indicators. Inspect issued token's aud claim. If aud contains both, attacker gets cross-API access. If aud is omitted, no resource binding at all.

token_exchange_audience_swap.description: RFC 8693 Token Exchange lets a token be exchanged for a new token (impersonation, delegation). Bug: backend doesn't validate `subject_token` audience matches the caller, allowing attacker to exchange a token they shouldn't have for a more-privileged one. Or `audience` parameter in the exchange accepts arbitrary values without RP-level allowlist.

probes.description: Exchange a low-privilege token for one targeting a high-privilege audience. If accepted, no per-audience authorization check on exchange.

oauth_mixup_attack.description: Mix-up: attacker tricks client into sending code/token to attacker AS, then attacker forwards to legitimate AS. Detected via iss parameter mismatch or absent iss in token response.

probes.description: Send token request with iss claim pointing at attacker AS. Vulnerable client doesn't validate iss against the AS metadata it used.

oauth_audience_confusion.description: Send token issued for Audience A to Resource Server B. Vulnerable RS doesn't validate aud claim and accepts any signed token from the same IdP.

probes.description: Use a token minted for a different audience. Operator captures victim token from a sister app, replays here.

jwks_kid_swap.description: JWT signature verification via JWKS — kid header tells verifier which key to use. If verifier fetches JWKS from URL in kid, attacker hosts own JWKS with matching kid.

probes.description: Kid header injection — verifier fetches JWKS from attacker URL
Kid path-traversal — read alternative JWKS from disk / unintended endpoint

redirect_uri_parser_quirks.description: redirect_uri parser bypass — fragment, userinfo, path-segment confusion, fake-port, urlencoded path. Many AS implementations are looser than RFC requires.

probes.description: Userinfo trick — many parsers see authority=legit.example.com but actual host=attacker.tld
Substring-match bypass — redirect_uri startsWith / contains check
Fragment-confusion — parser splits at # but UA sends whole string
Double-decode bypass — server decodes %2F twice, attacker tail wins

jwks_url_external_swap.description: OAuth/OIDC server takes JWKS endpoint URL from request (Kafka SASL OAuthBearer pattern; some IdPs). Swap to attacker-controlled JWKS → forge any token.

probes.description: Submit attacker JWKS URL — server fetches attacker keys → Collaborator hit confirms

par_request_uri_attacker_controlled_2025.description: PAR (Pushed Authorization Requests, RFC 9126) when supported but server allows fetch of request_uri from an attacker-controlled origin. Attacker submits /par to attacker server, then redirects victim to /authorize?request_uri=https://attacker/par-result — server fetches attacker-supplied parameters, overriding user intent. 2025 H2 PortSwigger research class.

probes.description: Submit attacker request_uri to /authorize — auth server fetches it; Collaborator hit confirms server-side fetch of attacker URI.

dpop_nonce_binding_skipped_2025.description: DPoP (RFC 9449) when server accepts proofs without enforcing nonce binding. Attacker captures victim DPoP proof, replays it at a different endpoint or after the iat window. 2025 H2 IETF OAuth WG erratum class.

probes.description: Replay a captured DPoP-bearing request after stripping the DPoP header — server should reject; if 200, DPoP not enforced. Alternative: replay across endpoint / after iat window.

description: . v1.0 chain-attack contexts: mix-up, audience confusion, JWKS swap, redirect_uri parser quirks (per draft-ietf-oauth-security-topics).
